entries.label: Honorius
entries.hint: Inherited throne age 10 on death of father Theodosius I; never led an army.
entries.note: Coin evidence: solidus series minted Ravenna/Mediolanum. Court relocated to Ravenna 402.

entries.label: Constantius III
entries.hint: General who married Galla Placidia, then muscled Honorius into making him co-emperor.
entries.note: Co-emperor with Honorius. Copper and gold coinage confirms brief reign. Died Aug 421.

entries.label: Valentinian III
entries.hint: Crowned age 6; real power held first by his mother Galla Placidia, then by general Aetius.
entries.note: Ivory diptych of consul Probus 406, later coins depict Valentinian. Murdered 16 Mar 455 — Hydatius Chronicle records date.

entries.label: Petronius Maximus
entries.hint: Wealthy senator who organised Valentinian III's assassination, then seized the throne.
entries.note: 11-week reign. Solidus coinage confirmed. Mob-killed 31 May 455 per Prosper of Aquitaine.

entries.label: Avitus
entries.hint: Gallic aristocrat elevated by Visigoth king Theoderic II; deposed by general Ricimer.
entries.note: Sidonius Apollinaris composed panegyric for him (Carmen VII, 456 AD). Deposed Oct 456.

entries.label: Majorian
entries.hint: Last Western emperor to mount serious military campaigns; murdered by strongman Ricimer.
entries.note: Seven novels (imperial laws) survive bearing his name and exact dates. Murdered 7 Aug 461.

entries.label: Libius Severus
entries.hint: Puppet installed by Ricimer; not recognised by Constantinople.
entries.note: Sparse coinage. John of Antioch records him as figurehead.

entries.label: Anthemius
entries.hint: Eastern candidate sent by Leo I to restore Western authority; killed in civil war with Ricimer.
entries.note: Greek inscription from Rome survives. Sidonius Apollinaris: panegyric Carmen II delivered 1 Jan 468.

entries.label: Olybrius
entries.hint: Puppet of Ricimer; reigned barely seven months before dying of illness.
entries.note: Solidus coins minted. Malalas chronicle records death.

entries.label: Glycerius
entries.hint: Army commander elevated locally at Milan; deposed on arrival of Eastern-backed Julius Nepos.
entries.note: Deposed by Julius Nepos. Coins minted Milan only.

entries.label: Julius Nepos
entries.hint: Last emperor recognised by both East and West; fled to Dalmatia when Orestes revolted.
entries.note: Solidus coins. Assassinated in Dalmatia 480 per Cassiodorus.

entries.label: Romulus Augustulus
entries.loc: Ravenna (court); exiled to Castellum Lucullanum, Bay of Naples
entries.hint: Boy emperor installed by his father Orestes; deposed 4 Sep 476 by Odoacer.
entries.note: Ennodius and Cassiodorus both record 4 Sep 476. Castellum Lucullanum = Pizzofalcone, Naples bay.

entries.label: Arcadius
entries.hint: Inherited Eastern throne age 17; dominated by court factions.
entries.note: Inscription base of obelisk, Hippodrome Constantinople (extant). Died 1 May 408.

entries.label: Theodosius II
entries.hint: Longest-reigning emperor of the century; compiled the Theodosian Code, built the great walls.
entries.note: Theodosian Code promulgated 15 Feb 438. Theodosian Walls 413 AD (still standing). Died horse accident 28 Jul 450.

entries.label: Marcian
entries.hint: Military officer who married empress Pulcheria to legitimise his claim; called Chalcedon.
entries.note: Council of Chalcedon 451 convened under him — acts survive. Ivory consular diptych 451 extant.

entries.label: Leo I
entries.hint: First emperor formally crowned by the Patriarch of Constantinople.
entries.note: Ceremony attested Theophanes Confessor. Novellae survive.

entries.label: Leo II
entries.hint: Seven-year-old grandson of Leo I; died of illness before ruling alone.
entries.note: Gold coinage. Died of illness Nov 474.

entries.label: Zeno
entries.hint: Isaurian general; seized sole rule on son's death, twice exiled and restored.
entries.note: Henotikon edict 482 — text in Evagrius Scholasticus HE III.14.

entries.label: Battle of Pollentia
entries.hint: Stilicho checks Alaric's Visigoths in northern Italy; fought on Easter Sunday.
entries.note: Claudian's poem De Bello Gothico composed 402 — primary near-contemporary source.

entries.label: Rhine crossing
entries.hint: Vandals, Alans and Suebi cross the frozen Rhine, breaching the western frontier for good.
entries.note: Jerome Letter 123 (written 409) describes devastation. Hydatius begins his chronicle from this event.

entries.label: Sack of Rome by Alaric
entries.hint: Visigoths under Alaric sack Rome — first time the city falls to an enemy in 800 years.
entries.note: Jerome Letters 127 & 128 (written 411) react directly. Augustine begins City of God partly in response.

entries.label: Battle of the Catalaunian Plains
entries.loc: Catalaunian Plains, Champagne, NE Gaul (near Châlons)
entries.hint: Aetius and allied Visigoths halt Attila's invasion of Gaul; Visigoth king Theodoric I killed.
entries.note: Jordanes Getica 197–218; Hydatius Chronicle §150. One of the largest battles of late antiquity.

entries.label: Attila invades Italy
entries.hint: Attila destroys Aquileia; Pope Leo I meets him at the Mincio and he withdraws.
entries.note: Hydatius §146; Prosper of Aquitaine records Leo's meeting. Attila dies 453.

entries.label: Vandal Sack of Rome
entries.hint: Gaiseric's Vandals loot Rome for 14 days; Pope Leo I negotiates against burning.
entries.note: Prosper of Aquitaine (primary contemporary) records 14-day sack.

entries.label: Majorian's fleet destroyed
entries.hint: Vandals intercept and burn the Roman fleet at Cartagena before it can reach Africa.
entries.note: John of Antioch fr.201.5 records it. Ends Majorian's reconquest plans.

entries.label: Battle of Cape Bon
entries.hint: Combined East-West fleet of ~1,100 ships destroyed by Vandal fire ships.
entries.note: Procopius BV I.6. Massive financial blow; effectively ends attempts to retake Africa.

entries.label: Odoacer deposes Romulus
entries.hint: Germanic foederati revolt at Pavia; Odoacer exiles the last Western emperor.
entries.note: Marcellinus Comes records date. Odoacer sends imperial regalia to Constantinople.

entries.label: Odoacer vs. Zeno (diplomatic standoff)
entries.hint: Odoacer rules Italy while nominally acknowledging Eastern overlordship.
entries.note: Cassiodorus Variae provide correspondence framework.

entries.label: Theodosian Walls constructed
entries.hint: Massive double land-wall encircling Constantinople, still standing today.
entries.note: Construction inscriptions survive in situ (Istanbul). Inner wall 5m thick, 12m high.

entries.label: Theodosian Code promulgated
entries.hint: First systematic compilation of Roman imperial law — 16 books of edicts.
entries.note: Senate readings recorded in acta — transcripts survive (Gesta Senatus). Codex Vossianus (Leiden) key MS.

entries.label: Majorian: Novel I — military reform
entries.hint: Reforms conscription and addresses mass desertion in a crumbling Western army.
entries.note: Survives in manuscript. One of seven surviving Majorian Novellae, each precisely dated.

entries.label: Majorian: Novel VII — tax relief
entries.hint: Attempts to ease the crushing tax burden on the curial class.
entries.note: Addresses tax burden on curiales. Explicitly dated. Survives in Novellarum Corpus.

entries.label: Henotikon edict (Zeno)
entries.hint: Zeno's compromise formula trying to paper over the East's Christological split.
entries.note: Full text in Evagrius Scholasticus HE III.14 (verbatim). Triggers Acacian Schism (484–519).

entries.label: Acacian Schism declared
entries.hint: Pope Felix III excommunicates Patriarch Acacius; East and West formally split.
entries.note: Letter of excommunication survives (Epistola XVI in papal register). Schism lasts 35 years.

entries.label: Notitia Dignitatum compiled
entries.loc: Western and Eastern imperial chanceries (Rome / Constantinople)
entries.hint: Illustrated register of all military and civil offices of the entire Roman state.
entries.note: Survives in medieval copies of a lost Carolingian original.

entries.label: Council of Ephesus I
entries.hint: 200+ bishops condemn Nestorius's teaching that Mary was not 'Mother of God'.
entries.note: Acts survive in Greek and Latin manuscripts. Nestorius deposed.

entries.label: Council of Ephesus II ('Robber Synod')
entries.hint: Packed council reverses Ephesus I — Pope Leo calls it a 'robber synod'.
entries.note: Pope Leo I's Tomus ad Flavianum (449) rejected here. Overturned at Chalcedon.

entries.label: Council of Chalcedon
entries.hint: Defines Christ as fully divine and fully human — the foundational Christological formula.
entries.note: 451 bishops. Acts survive in full (Concilium Chalcedonense, ACO II).

entries.label: Pope Leo I: Tomus ad Flavianum
entries.hint: Leo's letter defining the 'two natures' of Christ, acclaimed at Chalcedon 451.
entries.note: Survives in hundreds of manuscripts. Foundation of Chalcedonian Christology.

entries.label: Jerome dies
entries.hint: Translator of the Latin Vulgate Bible; his death ends the last great Latin Church Father.
entries.note: Vulgate (completed c.405) — earliest copy: Codex Amiatinus (c.692).

entries.label: Patrick's mission to Ireland
entries.hint: Romano-British bishop establishes Christianity in Ireland; two texts in his own hand survive.
entries.note: Confessio and Epistola ad Coroticum internally datable to mid-5th c. No external anchor confirmed.

entries.label: Missorium of Theodosius I
entries.loc: Found: Almendralejo, Spain · Now: Real Academia de la Historia, Madrid
entries.hint: 74cm silver dish commemorating Theodosius's tenth regnal year; depicts the imperial court.
entries.note: Inscription confirms 388. Found 1847.

entries.label: Diptych of Stilicho
entries.hint: Ivory consular diptych portrait of the Western generalissimo with his wife and son.
entries.note: Key image of a non-imperial power-holder in late Roman court dress.

entries.label: Mausoleum of Galla Placidia
entries.hint: Small cross-shaped mausoleum with the oldest and best-preserved mosaic programme in Ravenna.
entries.note: Still standing. Mosaic programme intact.

entries.label: Baptistery of the Orthodox
entries.hint: Octagonal baptistery with a vivid dome mosaic of Christ's baptism.
entries.note: Inscription of Bishop Neon survives inside. Begun c.400, completed c.450.

entries.label: Column of Arcadius base reliefs
entries.hint: Triumphal column recording Gothic campaigns; column lost 1715 but pre-modern drawings survive.
entries.note: Freshfield Album drawings (1574), Trinity College Cambridge.

entries.label: Projecta Casket (Esquiline Treasure)
entries.loc: Found: Esquiline Hill, Rome · Now: British Museum, London
entries.hint: Silver-gilt wedding casket with pagan and Christian imagery, from a wealthy Roman household.
entries.note: BM 1866,0520.1. Inscription 'SECUNDE ET PROJECTA VIVATIS IN CHRISTO'.

entries.label: Sarcophagus of Stilicho
entries.hint: Marble sarcophagus likely from Stilicho's family; shows Christian funerary art for the elite.
entries.note: Shows biblical scenes — example of Christian aristocratic funerary art.

entries.label: Augustine: City of God
entries.hint: 22-book rebuttal of pagans who blamed Christianity for Rome's fall.
entries.note: Begun after 410 Sack. Oldest manuscript: uncial fragments 5th–6th c.

entries.label: Orosius: Seven Books Against the Pagans
entries.loc: Written in Hispania (Braga region) and/or Bethlehem
entries.hint: First universal history from a Christian perspective, written at Augustine's request.
entries.note: Explicit date: book 7 written 'in the 1,164th year of the city' = 417 AD.

entries.label: Hydatius: Chronicle
entries.hint: Bishop in NW Spain; eyewitness to Vandal raids — our main source for 5th-century Iberia.
entries.note: Single 9th-c. copy survives (Madrid, Real Academia de la Historia).

entries.label: Prosper of Aquitaine: Chronicle
entries.loc: Rome (later career, papal court of Leo I)
entries.hint: Court insider who updates Jerome's chronicle; contemporary for Italian events from 425.
entries.note: Survives in many MSS. Key for Italian events.

entries.label: Priscus: History (fragments)
entries.hint: Diplomat who visited Attila's court in 449; his firsthand description is irreplaceable.
entries.note: Survives in fragments (Exc. de Legationibus). Describes Attila's wooden palace and feasts.

entries.label: Sidonius Apollinaris: Letters & Poems
entries.hint: Bishop of Clermont; 9 books of letters are an eyewitness record of Gaul's fall to Visigoths.
entries.note: MSS from 9th c. onward. Carmen II (panegyric to Anthemius, 468) datable within days.

entries.label: Olympiodorus of Thebes: History (fragments)
entries.loc: Author from Thebes, Egypt; embassy to Black Sea region
entries.hint: Egyptian diplomat who visited the Huns c.412; fragments cover the turbulent 407–425 period.
entries.note: Fragments in Photius Bibliotheca (cod. 80).

entries.label: Contraction of Western tax base
entries.loc: Western Empire broadly (Africa, Hispania, Gaul lost progressively)
entries.hint: Loss of Africa, Spain and Gaul to barbarian kingdoms strips the Western court of most tax revenue.
entries.note: Documented through Majorian's Novellae (458). Jones, LRE (1964) synthesises papyrus and law evidence.

entries.label: Vandal seizure of North Africa
entries.loc: N. Africa: Tingitana (Morocco) to Carthage (mod. Tunis)
entries.hint: Vandals cross from Spain, take Carthage 439 — stripping Rome of its richest grain-producing province.
entries.note: Prosper of Aquitaine records fall of Carthage 439. Coins of Gaiseric minted Carthage from 439.

entries.label: Collapse of Western coinage
entries.loc: Western Empire (mints at Rome, Milan, Ravenna)
entries.hint: Western gold solidus production becomes erratic after 455; silver and bronze collapse almost entirely by 470s.
entries.note: Numismatic evidence: die studies show sharp drop in Western mint output post-455. Hendy, Studies in the Byzantine Monetary Economy (1985).

entries.label: Theodosian Walls engineering
entries.hint: The most sophisticated urban fortification of late antiquity — triple walls, 96 towers, a moat.
entries.note: Construction inscriptions survive in situ. Outer wall added after 447 earthquake. Engineering described in Chronicon Paschale.

entries.label: Roman road network in use
entries.hint: The road network — over 80,000 km — continued operating, documented in the Tabula Peutingeriana.
entries.note: Tabula Peutingeriana: medieval copy (Österreichische Nationalbibliothek, Vienna, Cod. 324) of a late-Roman itinerary map.

entries.label: Ravenna harbour and fleet base
entries.hint: Honorius's decision to base the Western court at Ravenna made its harbour the main Western naval base.
entries.note: Archaeological evidence: Classis excavations. Sidonius Apollinaris Letter I.5 describes port activity c.461.

entries.label: Codex replaces scroll (transition)
entries.hint: By 400 the codex (bound book) has almost entirely replaced the papyrus roll for Christian and legal texts.
entries.note: Statistical evidence from papyrus finds: Roberts & Skeat, The Birth of the Codex (1983).

entries.label: Hunnic displacement cascade
entries.loc: Pontic steppe → Danube frontier → W. Europe (broad region)
entries.hint: Hunnic westward expansion pushes entire Gothic peoples across Roman frontiers, triggering a chain reaction.
entries.note: Ammianus Marcellinus res gestae 31 records Goths crossing Danube 376. Physical evidence: Hunnic cauldrons found across Pontic steppe.

entries.label: Earthquake, Constantinople
entries.hint: Major quake collapses sections of the Theodosian Walls — repaired in 60 days with Attila's army approaching.
entries.note: Surviving inscription on restored tower records Prefect Cyrus rebuilt 57 towers in 60 days.

entries.label: Plague: Spain and Gaul
entries.hint: Epidemic outbreaks noted in two independent contemporary sources; pathogen unknown.
entries.note: Hydatius Chronicle §129 records 446 in Spain. Sidonius letter V.14 mentions c.467 in Gaul.

entries.label: Attila's death and Hunnic collapse
entries.hint: Attila dies the night of his wedding; the Hunnic empire immediately disintegrates.
entries.note: Jordanes Getica 254; Priscus fragment 24. Hunnic sons fight Battle of Nedao (454).

entries.label: Late Roman climatic shift (LALIA precursor)
entries.hint: Tree-ring and ice-core data suggest climatic instability beginning mid-5th c., possibly contributing to agricultural stress.
entries.note: Evidence: Büntgen et al. (2016), Science 352 — Alpine tree-ring records. LALIA onset c.536 but precursor instability visible from c.450.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Justinian's Novel 77 — theology meets criminal law
entries.hint: Justinian's Novel 77 explicitly connects same-sex acts to natural disasters (earthquakes, famine, plague) as divine punishment — the first law to make this causal theological argument, which will shape European legal thinking for over a millennium.
entries.note: Justinian, Novel 77 (538 CE): full Greek text survives in the Corpus Juris Civilis. Novel 141 (544) repeats and strengthens it. Boswell (1980) pp.171–173. The earthquake-sodomy causation: unique to this legislation; becomes a template for later medieval law. This is the most consequential single CATEGORICAL SHIFT in late antique legislation on same-sex acts.

entries.label: Galla Placidia — regent empress and political actor
entries.hint: Galla Placidia rules as regent for her son Valentinian III for 14 years — issuing laws, commanding armies through generals, and commissioning the Ravenna mosaics; her political authority is extensively documented in imperial legislation and court records.
entries.note: Imperial laws issued under her regency: Codex Theodosianus and later Novels. Oost (1968), Galla Placidia Augusta: the standard biography. Her Ravenna mausoleum (extant, UNESCO) as a monument to self-representation. Coins: she appears on solidi as Augusta — numismatic evidence of official status. McEvoy (2013), Child Emperor Rule in the Late Roman West.

entries.label: Female saints passing as monks — the transvestite saints
entries.hint: A genre of hagiography emerges: women living as male monks (Pelagia, Marinos/Mary, Eugenia) — these texts are not straightforward transgender evidence but document a cultural category of gender crossing that the Church simultaneously recorded and sanctified.
entries.note: Vita Pelagiae (Syriac and Greek, 5th c.): AASS October 8. Vita Marini (Latin, 5th c.): collected in PL 73. Anson (1974), The Female Transvestite in Early Monasticism, Viator 5. Davis (2002), Crossed Texts, Crossed Sex: Intertextuality and Gender in Early Christian Legends of Holy Women Disguised as Men. These texts are primarily prescriptive/literary but document the cultural legibility of gender crossing.

entries.label: Codex Theodosianus on enslaved persons — legal personhood denied
entries.hint: The Theodosian Code systematically codifies enslaved persons' legal non-personhood alongside the laws of the Christian empire — the co-existence of Christian theology (all souls equal before God) and legal chattel slavery is here most starkly documented.
entries.note: CTh Book 2 (on contracts) and Book 9 (on criminal law): multiple provisions treating enslaved persons as property. Harper (2011), Slavery in the Late Roman World. The theological contradiction: Augustine's nuanced position on slavery in City of God 19.15 vs. the legal code. This tension is itself a historical fact about the period.
